checks.a_analysis: The output was reviewed slide by slide against the source document. No fabricated paragraphs, sentences, or sections were found. Text present in the output has a direct basis in the source document. Although some text in Section 11 is duplicated (the 'Bonus Tip' paragraph), it is not fabricated from thin air but is a redundant extraction of existing text.

All URLs and contact references were verified against the source document. Key URLs like 'aka.ms/askMA', 'aka.ms/DisabilityAnswerDesk', 'aka.ms/eDAD', and 'aka.ms/accessibility101' are correctly present. The text for various resources and training links on pages 22-25 also matches the source document exactly.

The source document consists of 25 slides. All 25 slides are represented in the output document, with their primary headings and lists converted into sections and elements. While some specific content blocks within pages are missing (see C5), no entire page or major section is absent.

There are three primary tables in the source (Pages 3, 6, and 8). The 'What is Accessibility?' table (p3) has 4 columns and data representing 4 items, which matches the JSON row/cell count. The 'Productivity Tools by Category' table (p6) is converted into a 4-row table capturing all 6 categories. The 'Copilot' table (p8) correctly captures the 4 columns and their contents. No data rows are missing.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: On Page 15, 'Accessible links', a significant text box is missing from the output. The source includes a box containing the heading 'No one-size-fits-all', a paragraph regarding Microsoft's commitment to accessibility ('We believe accessibility needs to be a priority...'), and a link 'Learn more about accessibility at Microsoft'. None of this content appears in the output section, representing an omission of more than 50% of the text on that page. Additionally, several illustrative screenshots on Page 17 (SharePoint accessibility) are missing as image elements, though their captions are present.
checks.b_evidence_source: The text box on Page 15 contains: 'No one-size-fits-all', 'We believe accessibility needs to be a priority that drives transformative change. As we navigate the promise of AI to fuel accessibility innovation, we’re committed to advancing accessibility as a fundamental right.', and 'Learn more about accessibility at Microsoft'.
checks.b_evidence_output: The output for section 15 ('Accessible links') only contains the two bullet points: 'Describe the link destination clearly' and 'Avoid generic phrases like “click here”'.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text content was checked for meaning-altering changes. The wording in the converted lists and paragraphs follows the source document faithfully. Redundant duplication of text found in Section 11 does not alter the information provided, only its formatting.

checks.a_analysis: In the section 'Alt text' (corresponding to page 11 of the source), the 'Bonus Tip' paragraph and the associated image/alt text are duplicated in the output. The source document contains the tip 'Bonus Tip: This also applies to images in email, Word, PowerPoint, PDF, and other documents.' only once at the bottom right. The output lists this paragraph twice (elements at index 5 and 7 in that section) and provides a second image placeholder (index 6) with similar alt text.
checks.b_evidence_source: Bonus Tip: This also applies to images in email, Word, PowerPoint, PDF, and other documents.
checks.b_evidence_output: The text 'Bonus Tip: This also applies to images in email, Word, PowerPoint, PDF, and other documents.' appears twice in the 'Alt text' section (elements 5 and 7).

checks.a_analysis: The output generally follows a flat heading structure where slide titles are H1s and major sub-sections are H2s. While some labels that appear as headings in the source (e.g., 'Page elements' on page 18) are converted to paragraphs, the overall hierarchy is logical and adjusted for accessibility. No major skips (like H1 to H4) were observed.

Tables on pages 3, 6, and 8 are correctly identified. Specifically, the statistics on page 3 are appropriately modeled as a table with headers and data rows. The grid on page 6 is represented as a multi-row table which effectively preserves the relationship between categories and lists. No corruption of rows or columns was found.

On page 15 ('Accessible links'), the document organization is significantly compromised because a major structural block is missing. The source contains a text box on the right titled 'No one-size-fits-all' which includes a paragraph and a link. This callout is completely absent from the output, which only captures the two bullet points on the left. This omission materially alters the intended organization of the page.
checks.b_evidence_source: The callout box on the right side of Page 15: 'No one-size-fits-all... We believe accessibility needs to be a priority... Learn more about accessibility at Microsoft'.
checks.b_evidence_output: The output section 'Accessible links' terminates after the second paragraph ('Avoid generic phrases...'), omitting the 'No one-size-fits-all' content block.

checks.a_analysis: Lists throughout the document (pages 5, 6, 7, etc.) are generally converted correctly into unordered list objects. While the grid on page 6 is converted into a table, the items themselves are maintained as lists within cells, preserving their meaning.

A significant number of links visible in the source document are missing their URL functionality in the output. On pages 22 through 25, which consist of lists of links, the output renders the text and marks it as underlined, but fails to include the 'href' property in the JSON. Additionally, prominent URLs like 'aka.ms/askMA' on page 20 are rendered as plain text rather than clickable link objects.
checks.b_evidence_source: Links on Pages 22-25 (e.g., 'Accessibility features for Windows', 'Accessibility tools for Word') and Page 20 (e.g., 'aka.ms/askMA').
checks.b_evidence_output: The corresponding JSON elements (e.g., in Section 'Productivity Tools Links') contain text spans without 'href' attributes.

checks.a_analysis: The conversion from a 25-page landscape slide deck to a single-column, long-form HTML document is an appropriate and accessible design choice. the logical flow of the original presentation is preserved, with sections following the same sequence as the source PDF. Multi-column slide layouts (such as on pages 3, 5, 6, and 8) have been successfully translated into accessible tables or lists that maintain the grouping and relationship of information.

The tables in the HTML output, specifically the 'Accessibility Statistics' table (from source page 3) and the 'Microsoft 365 Copilot' features table (from source page 8), are rendered correctly. They use a clear grid format that allows a sighted user to easily distinguish between headers and data rows/columns. No visual corruption, overlapping text, or alignment issues were observed.

Several meaningful images that illustrate core concepts are missing from the HTML output. Specifically, the screenshot of a TED talk showing captions (source page 13), the three Teams status icons showing accessibility symbols (source page 14), and the illustrative example block for accessible links (source page 15) are all absent from the rendered HTML. These omissions degrade the informational value of their respective sections.
checks.b_evidence_source: Source PDF Page 13 shows a TED talk video screenshot; Page 14 shows three Teams status icons; Page 15 shows a 'No one-size-fits-all' example block.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML rendering for sections 'Captions and transcripts', 'Convey information', and 'Accessible links' is missing these illustrative images/blocks, showing only the surrounding text.
checks.b_evidence_location: Sections: Captions and transcripts (page 13), Convey information (page 14), Accessible links (page 15)

checks.a_analysis: A significant instance of content duplication occurs in the body of the rendered HTML. In the 'Alt text' section, the 'Bonus Tip' sentence is repeated twice consecutively. This is not a case of navigational duplication (like a heading appearing in both TOC and body), but actual body text appearing twice in the same content block.
checks.b_evidence_source: Bonus Tip: This also applies to images in email, Word, PowerPoint, PDF, and other documents.
checks.b_evidence_output: The sentence 'Bonus Tip: This also applies to images in email, Word, PowerPoint, PDF, and other documents.' is rendered twice, one after the other.
checks.b_evidence_location: Alt text section, element following the 'Everything you need to know...' link (Page 11)
